coredata.dc:title: Smart dual imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD for selective and simultaneous analysis of vanillylmandelic acid and 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid carcinoid cancer biomarkers using graphene quantum dots coated with dual molecularly imprinted polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Somnet, Kanpitcha
Chimjarn, Supansa
Wanram, Surasak
Jarujamrus, Purim
Nacapricha, Duangjai
Lieberzeit, Peter A.
Amatatongchai, Maliwan
coredata.dc:description: Measuring the levels of the biomarkers vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) and 5-Hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (5-HIAA) is a valuable tool for clinical diagnosis not only of neuroblastoma or carcinoid syndrome, but also of essential hypertension, depression, migraine, and Tourette's syndrome. Herein, we explore using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) as novel dual-imprinted sensors for selective and simultaneous determination of VMA and 5-HIAA in urine and plasma samples. The dual-MIP was successfully coated on the GQDs core via co-polymerization of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), acting as functional and cross-linking monomers, respectively. In addition, we successfully created the dual imprinted VMA and 5-HIAA shell on the GQDs' core via a one-pot synthesis. We fabricated a facile and ready-to-use Origami three-dimensional electrochemical paper-based analytical device (Origami 3D-ePAD) for simultaneous determination of VMA and 5-HIAA using a GQDs@dual-MIP modified graphene electrode (GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE). The Origami 3D-ePAD was designed to form a voltammetric cell on a three-layer foldable sheet with several advantages. For example, they were quickly assembled and enhanced the device's physical durability with the hydrophobic backup sheet. The developed dual imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD leads to substantially enhanced sensitivity and selectivity to electrochemical signal amplification generated from increasing the electrode-specific surface area, electrocatalytic activity, and the large numbers of dual imprinted sites for VMA and 5-HIAA detection. The synthetic recognition sites are highly selective for 5-HIAA and VMA molecules with an imprinting factor of 8.46 and 7.10, respectively. Quantitative analysis relying on square wave voltammetry reveals excellent linear dynamic ranges of around 0.001–25 μM, with detection limits of 0.023 nM for 5-HIAA and 0.047 nM for VMA (3Sb, n = 3). The Origami 3D-ePAD provides high accuracy and precision (i.e., recovery values of 5-HIAA ranged from 82.98 to 98.40 %, and VMA ranged from 83.28 to 104.39 %), and RSD less than 4.37 %) in urine and plasma samples without any evidence of interference. Hence, it is well suited as a facile and ready-to-use disposable device for point-of-care testing. It is straightforward, cost-effective, reproducible, and stable. Furthermore, it allows for rapid analysis (analysis time ∼20s) useful in medical diagnosis and other relevant fields.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell. (A) FE-SEM images, EDS investigation of elemental composition, and mapping analysis mixture of C, O, N and Si elements. (B) TEM images of GQDs@dual-MIP at low-and high-magnification.

(A) Nyquist plot observed for (a) bare SPGE, (b) GQDs/SPGE, (c) GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE, and (d) GQDs@NIP/SPGE in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- over the frequency range 0.1 Hz–100 kHz; amplitude = 17 mV. Inset; Randles equivalent circuit model; here R
s , C
dl , R
ct , and Z
w stand for the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte (R
s ), double layer capacitance (C
dl ), electron transfer resistance (R
ct ), and Warburg impedance (Z
w ). (B) CV curves of 50 μM 5-HIAA and VMA on bare SPGE, GQDs/SPGE, GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE, and GQDs@NIP/SPGE in 0.04 M B–R buffer pH 2.0 supporting solution.

(A) Origami 3D-ePAD for the simultaneous analysis of-HIAA and VMA using SWV in 0.04 M B–R buffer pH 2.0 at different concentrations (0.001, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.10, 1, 3, 5,10, 15, and 25 μM) and the corresponding linear calibration curves (B and C) for 5-HIAA and VMA.
The selectivity of the imprinted (GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE) and non-imprinted (GQDs@NIP/SPGE) sensors for (A) 5-HIAA and (B) VMA with foreign interferences.
The synthesis process of GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell particles comprising dual 5-HIAA and VMA imprinting sites.

Fabrication of the Origami 3D-ePAD, (A) Fabrication process: the three foldable layers-sheet is fabricated by adding the hydrophobic barrier followed by screen-printing of three electrodes on the middle layer. The figure illustration is of the ready-to-use Origami 3D-ePAD (B) Digital image of six devices fabricated using a single screen on a paper substrate. The device dimensions are 3.8 cm × 5.7 cm.
The proposed electrochemical oxidation mechanism of 5-HIAA and VMA on the GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE.
Comparison of the developed dual-MIP sensor with other methods [1,4,48–60].

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Electrode: GCE = glassy carbon electrode, ITO=Indium tin oxide electrode, PG=Pyrolytic graphite electrode, SPCE=Screen-printed carbon electrode, SPGE=Screen-printed graphene electrode, BDD=Boron doped diamond electrode, CCE=Carbon composite film electrode, BPPG = Edge-plane pyrolytic graphite electrode and PGE=Pencil graphite electrode as working electrodes.

Modifier: p(P3CA)/Pt/f = poly(pyrrole-3-carboxylic acid) on platinized carbon nanofibers, MIPPy = molecularly imprinted polypyrrole, AuNPs = gold nanoparticles, p-BG/GNRs = graphene nanoribbons/poly-bromocresol green, MMIC = magnetic molecularly imprinted catalytic polymer, MWCNT-Pt = multi-walled carbon nanotube and platinum nanoparticles, MIP = molecularly imprinted polymer, PASA = poly(4-Aminosalicylic acid).

Technique: CV = cyclic voltammetry, SSWV = stripping square wave voltammetry, DPV = differential pulse voltammetry, OSWV = osteryoung square wave voltammetry, SWV = square wave voltammetry, FIA = flow injection analysis, Amp = Amperometry.

head.ce:title: Smart dual imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD for selective and simultaneous analysis of vanillylmandelic acid and 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid carcinoid cancer biomarkers using graphene quantum dots coated with dual molecularly imprinted polymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Measuring the levels of the biomarkers vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) and 5-Hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (5-HIAA) is a valuable tool for clinical diagnosis not only of neuroblastoma or carcinoid syndrome, but also of essential hypertension, depression, migraine, and Tourette's syndrome. Herein, we explore using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) as novel dual-imprinted sensors for selective and simultaneous determination of VMA and 5-HIAA in urine and plasma samples. The dual-MIP was successfully coated on the GQDs core via co-polymerization of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), acting as functional and cross-linking monomers, respectively. In addition, we successfully created the dual imprinted VMA and 5-HIAA shell on the GQDs' core via a one-pot synthesis. We fabricated a facile and ready-to-use Origami three-dimensional electrochemical paper-based analytical device (Origami 3D-ePAD) for simultaneous determination of VMA and 5-HIAA using a GQDs@dual-MIP modified graphene electrode (GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE). The Origami 3D-ePAD was designed to form a voltammetric cell on a three-layer foldable sheet with several advantages. For example, they were quickly assembled and enhanced the device's physical durability with the hydrophobic backup sheet. The developed dual imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD leads to substantially enhanced sensitivity and selectivity to electrochemical signal amplification generated from increasing the electrode-specific surface area, electrocatalytic activity, and the large numbers of dual imprinted sites for VMA and 5-HIAA detection. The synthetic recognition sites are highly selective for 5-HIAA and VMA molecules with an imprinting factor of 8.46 and 7.10, respectively. Quantitative analysis relying on square wave voltammetry reveals excellent linear dynamic ranges of around 0.001–25 μM, with detection limits of 0.023 nM for 5-HIAA and 0.047 nM for VMA (3Sb, n = 3). The Origami 3D-ePAD provides high accuracy and precision (i.e., recovery values of 5-HIAA ranged from 82.98 to 98.40 %, and VMA ranged from 83.28 to 104.39 %), and RSD less than 4.37 %) in urine and plasma samples without any evidence of interference. Hence, it is well suited as a facile and ready-to-use disposable device for point-of-care testing. It is straightforward, cost-effective, reproducible, and stable. Furthermore, it allows for rapid analysis (analysis time ∼20s) useful in medical diagnosis and other relevant fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (5-HIAA) and vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) are crucial biomarkers excreted in urine and plasma. 5-HIAA is a primary metabolite of serotonin. Hence, certain pathological condiations lead to changed 5-HIAA levels, including carcinoid tumors of enterochromaffin cells of the small intestine. Those release large amounts of serotonin, resulting in a significant increase in 5-HIAA urinary excretion [1]. On the other hand, VMA is the major end product of the catecholamine metabolism [2]. The levels of 5-HIAA and VMA secretion are useful to predict neuroendocrine tumors such as neuroblastoma, pheochromocytoma, and carcinoid tumors, cancers, as well as various disorders: high level of 5-HIAA in body fluids are linked to the diagnosis of hypertension, depression, migraine, and Tourette's syndrome, among others. High VMA levels relate to Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, suicide attempts, and posttraumatic stress disorder [1–4]. Commonly, 5-HIAA levels in healthy persons are reported in the range of 17.8–58.3 μM in urine [4,5]. Plasma 5-HIAA concentrations in the healthy individuals are reported as ∼0.115 μM. Previous studies found that its concentration increases significantly in cancer pateints [5,7]. VMA concentrations in healthy humans are 11.6–28.7 μM in urine and 0.022–0.0736 μM in plasma [4,6].

Numerous analytical techniques have been developed to determine 5-HIAA and VMA individually, including fluorescence [8,9], immunoassay [10], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [11] for VMA. Methods for analyzing 5-HIAA include electrochemical methods [1,4,12,13], fluorescence [14] and chemiluminescence [15]. Traditional ELISA kits may suffer from drawbacks including the high cost of antigens/antibodies or enzymes, time-consuming production, poor stability, limited availability and shelf time, and the need for stringent storage conditions. To analyze samples containing high concentrations of matrix compounds, such as urine and plasma, several chromatography-based techniques have been developed to determine 5-HIAA [16–18] and VMA [3,19–22] individually as well as simultaneously [23,24]. Despite the advantages of adequate sensitivity and selectivity, the requirement for expensive laboratory facilities, well-trained operators, and time-consuming sample preparation and unnecessary instruments are problems that need addressing. There is an urgent and vital need for accurate, cost-effective, and rapid measurement technologies capable of detecting 5-HIAA and VMA cancer-specific biomarkers, especially at very low concentrations in the early stages of cancer.

To meet this need, this work explores sensors relying on core-shell molecularly imprinted polymer (core-shell MIP) and an electrochemical paper-based analytical device (ePAD). MIP serve as antibody mimics in molecular recognitions due to their high selectivity. Further seminal advantages include straightforward manufacturing, high thermal and physical ruggedness, and possible customization for specific analytes at low cost. Core-shell MIP nanoparticles comprise a core coated with a thin MIP film that usually is the result of surface imprinting. Compared to thin films, their main advantages lie in faster response, increased electrocatalytic response when using an appropriate core material, and ruggedness. These properties make them interesting for the use in ePADs [25,26]. The benefits of this are: a cost-effective, user-friendly flexible platform, compatible with a wide array of patterning methods, lightweight, easily disposable, and versatile for modification with various functional groups for performing analyte detection [25,26]. The present work utilizes a novel core-shell MIP integrated into a three-dimensional ePAD (3D-ePAD) design for 5-HIAA and VMA based on the Origami concept to satisfy the demands mentioned previously.

Recently, we proposed a somewhat similar concept for simultaneously determining different cancer biomarkers, namely 3-NT and 8-OHdG [25] as well as 3-NT and 4-NQO [26]. Both approaches use dual imprinting on silica nanospheres decorated with silver nanoparticles (SiO2@AgNPs) [25] and graphene quantum-dot capped gold nanoparticles (GQDs-AuNPs) [26]. Unlike the previous work, we select GQDs as the efficient core for 5-HIAA and VMA MIP shells due to it has high surface area and conductivity, allows for high-speed electron transport due to quantum confinement, and a better interaction using π–π conjugation and surface groups on its surface, resulting in increased numbers of imprinting sites. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on creating dual-recognition site MIP on the surface of a GQD. The present work introduces such GQDs-MIP core shell to determine VMA and 5-HIAA simultaneously with an ePAD design. The Origami 3D-ePAD is designed to form a voltammetric cell on a three-layer foldable sheet with several modifications to enhance the device's physical durability with the hydrophobic backup sheet, and make it easy to assembled.

All chemicals were of analytical grade; all solutions were prepared in deionized water (DI water) (Waters Pro-PS, USA). The citric acid (C6H8O4, ∼99.0 %), acetic acid (CH3COOH, ∼99.5 %) and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Carlo Erba (Milan, Italy). Ammonia solution (25 %) was purchased from Merck. (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %) and vanillylmandelic acid (VMA 98 %) were obtained from Acros organics (Geel, Belgium). 5-Hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (5-HIAA, 98 %) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Heysham, United Kingdom). Ethanol and methanol were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Whatman No.1 (thickness 180 μm, pore size 11 μm) filter paper was purchased from Whatman International Ltd. (Maidstone, England).

In this work, we manufactured dual-imprinted Origami 3D-ePADs using a new design to form a voltammetric cell composed of three foldable layers using an in-house screen-printing process. Penguard enamel color (Jotun Thailand Ltd.) for screening the hydrophobic barrier was purchased from a building supply store. Graphene (SSG-1760A) and Ag/AgCl (SSS-2075) ink were obtained from Serve Science Company (Bangkok, Thailand). A hand-made, wooden-framed woven nylon-mesh screen (888.32 mesh, 60T) was obtained from a local screen shop (Ubon Ratchathani, Thailand).

The structure and content of the as-prepared nanocomposites were investigated using an FT-IR spectrometer (spectrum II, PerkinElmer, USA) with an operating range of 4000–500 cm−1. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded in the range of 300–800 nm to investigate the conversion of GQDs, using a double beam spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) with a 10 mm quartz cell (45 × 12.5 × 12.5 mm, volume 1500 μL). Fluorescence properties of the as-prepared GQDs were characterized using a spectrofluorometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer, USA). The morphologies and sizes of nanocomposites were studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (1230 JEOL, Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (JSM 7610F, JEOL, Japan), in conjunction with energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) (INCAx-Max20, OXFORD instruments, U.K.).

Our Origami 3D-ePAD constitutes a three-electrode voltammetric cell comprising a designed working electrode (WE, GQDs@dual-MIP modified SPGE, 4 mm diameter, active surface area around 0.126 cm2), a reference electrode (RE, Ag/AgCl), and a counter electrode (CE, graphene ink). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were measured with an EmStat3 potentiostat (5.0 × 6.7 × 2.8 cm) (PalmSens BV, The Netherlands) outfitted with "PSTrace for Windows" and multi-trace software. The Origami 3D-ePAD electrodes were linked to a potentiostat using alligator-clip connections, followed by adding 40 μL Britton–Robinson (B–R) buffer (0.04 M, pH = 2) as the supporting electrolyte to the detecting zone before performing CV or SWV measurements and utilized. CV was recorded using a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 across a potential range of 0.0 to +1.2 V. We selected SWV for simultaneous quantitative analysis of 5-HIAA and VMA. The optimized SWV conditions were: potential step = 25 mV, square wave amplitude = 20 mV, frequency = 5 Hz, and across a potential range of 0.0 to +1.0 V.

Furthermore, Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with a compact EmStat4S HR potentiostat (5.5 × 7.2 × 2.6 cm) (PalmSens BV, The Netherlands) equipped with multi-trace software plus impedance measurement mode. The impedance spectra of several modified electrodes were measured in 0.1 M aqueous KCl solution containing 5 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6] at a potential of 0.19 V throughout a frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz.

Scheme 1 illustrates the strategy to create dual 5-HIAA and VMA imprinting sites on the GDQs core. GQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid using a method adapted from our previous report [26]. Briefly, citric acid (6.00 g) was heated to 200 °C and melted to dark yellow-brown liquid (∼2 h). After that, 0.25 M NaOH (25 mL) was added dropwise to the resulting product under continuous stirring followed by further stirring for 30 min. The resulting product was purified in a dialysis bag for 24 h to obtain GQDs (∼200 mg mL−1). Next, we synthesized GQDs@dual-MIP via forming silica on the GQD surface using APTES as the functional monomer. Self-assembly of an organosilane coupling agent on the surface core from relied on our previous work [26], but replacing MPTMS (3-mercaptopropyl trimethoxysilne) applied there by APTES. APTES-modified GQDs were prepared by mixing purified GQD solution (5.0 mL, 25 mg mL−1) and APTES (375 μL) in a 20 mL vial and keeping at 40 °C for 30 min under stirring (Solution A). At the same time, solution B was prepared by dissolving VMA (9.9 mg, 0.050 mmol) and 5-HIAA (9.6 mg, 0.050 mmol) in ethanol (2.0 mL), followed by adding DI water (5.0 mL) and APTES monomer (23 μL, 0.100 mmol) under stirring at room temperature and further stirring for 1 h. After that, solution A was mixed with solution B while mechanically stirring at 60 °C for 30 min. Then, TEOS cross-linker (320 μL, 1.44 mmol) and NH3 (150 μL) were added to the resulting solution under continuous stirring. The mixture was aged for another 24 h under continuous stirring. Then, the synthesized particles were collected after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. To remove the template and any excess reagent absorbed on the polymer surface, the product was soaked in 10 mL methanol: acetic acid (9 : 1, v/v) mixture under stirring for 10 min, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Solvent extraction was performed duplicate to achieve template removal. The resulting products were dried in a desiccator to obtain the GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell particles. The control material-denoted GQDs@NIP (GQDs coated with non-imprinted polymer), was synthesized by the same procedure, but in the absence of the analytes (VMA and 5-HIAA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of origami 3D-ePAD for simultaneous determination of the 5-HIAA and VMA
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 2 illustrates the fabrication of the Origami 3D-ePAD for simultaneous determination of the tumor markers 5-HIAA and VMA. The designed pattern comprises of three foldable layers, namely: loading circular reservoir (top)/three-screen-printed electrodes (middle)/hydrophobic back-up (bottom). The sheet can be fabricated instantly by screen printing with i) Penguard enamel color to form a hydrophobic barrier and ii) inks to form electrodes. Scheme S1 shows the details of the Origami 3D-ePAD manufacture (A) reservoir construction and (B) electrode construction via screen-printing through the wooden-frame mesh screen (888.32 mesh, 60T). The design reservoir pattern (Scheme S1A) consists of two circular reservoirs (1.0 cm in diameter). Penguard enamel color mixture (color: hardener: thinner in a ratio of 4 : 1:2 v/v) was screened onto Whatman No.1 filter paper (3.8 cm × 5.7 cm) to construct the reservoir. The printed form was then kept at 60 °C for 20 min to generate a long-lasting hydrophobic coating. Next, three electrodes for electrochemical detection were created using an in-house screen-printing process as shown in Scheme S1B. Graphene ink was screen printed onto the paper with a squeegee within the circular reservoir using a mesh screen to generate a counter electrode (CE) and a reference electrode connection (REC). Then, the working electrode (WE)was formed by printing graphene ink mixed with GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell in a 100 : 3 m/m ratio on the paper. Lastly, the reference electrode (RE) was printed on the paper sheet using Ag/AgCl ink. The paper was heated to 60 °C for 20 min after each printing stage to cure the ink.

To construct the voltammetric cell, the top layer (the right-hand reservoir) was cut by scissors along the back dotted line (Scheme 2A) to allow the device to contact the alligator clips. The top layer was connected to the middle layer by folding the device along the crease, doubling over on itself and sealing via the double-sided adhesive tape. The bottom layer was folded backward to give a hydrophobic backup layer, preventing the solution from seeping out underneath. This design enhances the physical durability of the Origami 3D-ePAD. As can be seen in the digital image of Scheme 2B, six devices can be fabricated reproducibly by a single process. The Origami 3D-ePAD shows good flexibility and mechanical stability, with excellent physical durability, it is cost-effective, suitable for large-scale production, and good for point-of-care analysis.

We analyzed 5-HIAA and VMA simultaneously in urine and plasma samples to evaluate the performance of the proposed Origami 3D-ePAD, with permission from the Human Research Ethical Committee of Ubon Ratchathani University (UBU-REC-111/2565). Healthy volunteer students donated urine and plasma samples at the Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University. To obtain trustworthy data, specific considerations were made during sample collecting consisting of avoiding caffeinated drinks (tea and coffee), drugs, and serotonin-containing foods (bananas, pineapple, plums, eggplant, avocado, kiwi, nuts, tomatoes, and juice of all fruits) for 48 h before and during specimen collection for this research. Urine samples were collected and stored at 4 οC. Whole blood samples were first centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min; the plasma samples were collected from the supernatant. Next, the supernatant and urine samples were filtered through a 0.22-mm nylon membrane. Subsequently, the plasma and urine samples were appropriately diluted (from 10-to 20-fold) with 0.04 M B–R buffer (pH 2.0) before determining the 5-HIAA and VMA content.

Plasma samples from cancer patients were also analyzed to assess the device for diagnostics. Plasma samples (∼500 μL) were obtained from four colorectal cancer patients (P6–P9) whose diagnoses were confirmed histopathologically with permission from the Ethical Committee of Ubon Ratchathani Cancer Hospital, Thailand (No. EC 003/2020).

Samples were analyzed using the developed Origami 3D-ePAD. Analysis results of 5-HIAA and VMA obtained were compared and validated against a standard HPLC method reported by amending modified K. Jiang's method [27]. Figure S7 in SI summarizes the details of the HPLC method used.

We used UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy, FI-IR, TEM, and FE-SEM combined with EDS to investigate the surface morphology and element composition of the synthesized GQDs. Figure S1A shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the purified GQDs. The UV spectrum (blue line, Figure S1A) exhibits a significant absorption band at 365 nm due to the n → π* transition of surface functional groups (edge state, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl) [28,29]. The fluorescence spectra in Figure S1A (pink line) show maximum fluorescence emission (λem) at 460 nm when excited at 365 nm, which is consistent with the previous reports [30,31]. The inset photographs (Figure S1A) show the yellow transparent solution of GQDs taken under visible light (left) and blue luminescence under 365 nm UV light (right).

FTIR spectra were recorded to investigate and characterize the chemical bonding states of GQDs, which are shown in Figure S1B. The characteristic absorption peaks of FTIR are at 3340, 1715, and 1287 cm−1, respectively. These peaks are assigned to stretching and bending vibration of - OH, stretching of CO, and stretching of C – O in carboxylic acid groups, respectively [32–34]. In addition, characteristic absorption bands of GQDS were also observed, for instance: CC stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1, O–H deformation vibration at 1397 cm−1, and phenolic hydroxyl group stretching of C - OH groups at 1218 cm−1, which are similar to previous works [32–35].

GQDs morphology, size, and elemental composition were examined by TEM and FE-SEM combined with EDS. TEM image of typical GQDs (Figure S1C) shows a monodispersed and spherical structure with an average particle size of 5 nm (count = 30). The EDS spectrum (Figure S1D) revealed that the GQDs contain C (76.94 wt%) and O (23.06 wt%) atoms. The atomic ratio of C/O is approximately 3.34/1, indicating that C is the dominant element in the GQDs. The shape and size observed agree well with previous works [26,34,35].

FE-SEM images in Fig. 1 A show rough surfaces of GQDs@dual-MIP with the particles’ diameter as approximately 102 ± 7 nm. This indicates the formation of a dual-MIP coatings on the GQD; the rough surfaces occur after removing the template molecules. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 1A) reveals the presence of C (28.29 wt%), O (30.90 wt%), N (2.84 wt%), and Si (37.97 wt%). This demonstrates the binding of the GQDs core to the organic component of the polymer matrix generated from APTES and TEOS. Furthermore, elemental mapping (Fig. 1A) shows an equal distribution of C, O, N, and Si elements on the GQDs@dual-MIP. These findings confirm the formation of GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell particles. The corresponding TEM results (Fig. 1B) exhibit a spherical shape and uniform nanosphere with a mean diameter of approximately 147 ± 2 nm (count = 10). Moreover, a close-up view of the final particles clearly shows that each GQD core (dark) is encapsulated in a dual-MIP shell (bright) to form a nano core-shell structure.

We used FTIR to confirm the formation of 5-HIAA and VMA recognition sites and successful template removal from in the polymermetric of the synthesized GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell. Figure S2 shows FTIR spectra of 5-HIAA (curve a), VMA (curve b), GQDs@dual-MIP before (curve c) and after (curve d) removal of the template molecules, and GQDs@NIP (curve e). GQDs@dual-MIP spectrum before template removal (curve c) reveals an absorption band of the characteristic peaks of 5-HIAA (star marked) and VMA (triangle marked), indicating that they are present in the polymer matrix. After template removal (curve d), these characteristic peaks disappear, proving successful template removal. Moreover, GQDs@NIP (curve e) and GQDs@dual-MIP after removal of the templates (curve d) display characteristic bands of poly APTES-co-TEOS at 2925, 1543, 1407, 1043, and 796 cm−1, which are ascribed to stretching vibrations of C–H, C–N, N–H, asymmetric and symmetric Si–O–Si, respectively [36]. All of the above results indicate that the amino-silane group of APTES had been incorporated into the GQDs@dual-MIP and, thus, confirms successful synthesis of the core-shell particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of origami 3D-ePAD by EIS and CV
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the electron transfer kinetics of both bare and modified SPGE electrodes using EIS. Fig. 2 A shows the EIS data obtained for a bare SPGE (curve a), GQDs/SPGE (curve b), GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE (curve c), and GQDs@NIP/SPGE (curve d). As can be seen, the lowest semicircular diameter results from the GQDs/SPGE (Rct = 650 Ω), which indicates the fastest electron-transfer kinetics of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe, compared to the bare SPGE (Rct = 1970 Ω), GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE (Rct = 1151 Ω) and GQDs@NIP/SPGE (Rct = 1475 Ω). The impedance of GQDs/SPGE was significantly lower than bare SPGE indicating the GQDs substantially enhance conductivity. The impedances of GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE and GQDs@NIP/SPGE were lower than bare SPGE, indicating the presence of the polymer films covering the GQDs core. Moreover, the impedance of the MIP-modified electrode was smaller than that of the NIP electrode, possibly because of the presence of imprinted cavities in the MIP film allowing for better diffusion of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-.

CV was performed to investigate the electrochemical behavior of 5-HIAA and VMA at the bare SPGE and modified SPGE (GQDs/SPGE, GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE, and GQDs@NIP/SPGE). As depicted in Fig. 2B, GQDs/SPGE provides higher anodic peak currents for 5-HIAA (3.5 times) and VMA (∼8 times) than bare SPGE. One can trace this back to the appreciable properties of GQDs, such as consisting of a single atomic layer of nano-sized graphite, high-speed electron transport of quantum confinement, an edge effect that directly affects electrical conductivity, high surface area, better interaction using π – π conjugation, and surface groups on the surface leading to improved electron transfer between GQDs and both target analytes [37,38]. This result illustrates that GQDs can accelerate the oxidation process. After encapsulating GQDs with dual-MIP (GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE), the anodic peak current significantly increased. Interestingly, the current obtained from GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE was larger than for either GQDs@NIP/SPGE or bare SPGE (inset of Fig. 2B). The highest electrochemical signal of 5-HIAA and VMA results from the MIP sensor (∼11 and ∼15 times larger than that obtained from bare SPGE), indicating that the GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell particle exhibits very high affinity for both target analytes. We assume that the ability of GQDs@dual-MIP to enhance the analyte signals is the consequence of selective recognition cavities formed in the polymer shell. The core-shell has high electrocatalytic activity and conductivity, as well as enhanced interaction with target analytes via the hydrogen bond, which improves sensitivity. The greater affinity of the MIP for analyte molecules observed in this study is in accord with previous reports [25,26,39]. The created specific recognition cavities on the GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE therefore enhance electrode properties in terms of sensitivity and selectivity.

Supporting information (SI), Section S1 illustrates the effect of GQDs loading, the mole ratio of template-to-monomer and the mole ratio of monomer-to-cross linker, as well as details for the polymerization conditions that affect the imprinting factor (as in Figure S3). As expressed in Eqn. (1), the imprinting factor (IF) is the ratio of sensor's current response obtained from MIP and NIP.

The most significant IF values confirm good imprinting of MIP. On the other hand, IF values close to 1 infer that the imprinting effect is almost insignificant. This means that the interaction between the MIP and the template is somewhat unspecific, and more involved in surface interactions than in any generated recognition cavities [40,41]. We investigated the polymerization conditions that provide the highest imprinting factor. Our results revealed that optimal conditions for synthesis are: (i) GQDs loading of 25 mg mL−1 (ii) the mole ratio of template-to-monomer (5-HIAA: VMA: APTES) at 1 : 1: 2 and (iii) monomer-to-cross linker (APTES: TEOS) at 2 : 28.8.

We investigated the detection mechanism on the Origami 3D-ePAD by examining the influence of pH on the electrocatalytic oxidation of 5-HIAA and VMA at the GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE over the range of pH 2.0 to 7.0 in 0.04 M B–R buffer (Figure S4).

Figure S4A shows the peak responses of 5-HIAA and VMA (P1 and P2) oxidation reactions. The effects of pH on anodic peak potential (Ep,a) and peak current (Ip,a) of 5-HIAA and VMA are shown in Figures S4B and S4C. The anodic peak currents (Ip,a) of both compounds gradually decrease with increasing pH. The respective peak potentials (Ep,a) shift toward less positive values with increasing pH. The linear relationships between Ep,a and pH can be expressed as

The observed slopes of 0.0500, 0.0510, and 0.0521 V/pH are close to the Nernstian slope of 0.0591 V/pH. The results clearly indicate that the electrooxidation of 5-HIAA and VMA on the GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE involves an equal number of protons and electrons.
The CVs of 50 μM 5-HIAA and VMA were recorded at different scan rates (ν) to investigate the number of electrons involved in the respective oxidation reactions (Figure S5A).

Figure S5B depicts a plot of 5-HIAA and VMA anodic peak currents (μA) increasing linearly with the square root of scan rate (mV1/2 s−1/2). Linear regression analysis provides a coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9915 for 5-HIAA, and 0.9944 for VMA reactions. This reveals that electrochemical oxidation of 5-HIAA and VMA on the electrode are typical diffusion-controlled processes.

Where E0 represents the formal potential, ks is the standard rate constant of the reaction, R is the gas constant (R = 8.314 J K−1 mol−1), T is room temperature (T = 298 K), F is the Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1), ν is the scan rate (mV s−1), α is the transfer coefficient, and n is the electron transfer number. We calculated the value of (1-α) n from the slopes obtained from 5-HIAA and VMA curves in Figure S5C. The α value was calculated according to the Bard and Faulkner in Eqn. (6) [43].

Here, Ep/2 is the oxidation potential where the current is at half its peak value. The transfer coefficient (α) for 5-HIAA, VMA for (P1), and VMA for (P2) were calculated to be 0.23, 0.33, and 0.22, respectively. The value of the electron transfer number (n) for 5-HIAA, VMA for (P1), and VMA for (P2) were calculated as 2.00 (∼2), 1.02 (∼1), and 1.03 (∼1), respectively. The reaction mechanism of 5-HIAA may thus involve two-electron transfers in Scheme 3 A. We attributed this single peak to irreversible oxidation of the hydroxyl group in the aromatic ring of 5-HIAA to form a ketone, which is similar to serotonin because they have a similar structure [44,45]. There are no current reports on the electrochemical oxidation mechanism of VMA at pH 2.0. However, one can make conclusions from the oxidation process of homovanillic acid, which also belongs to the catecholamine group and whose behaviors has been reported at pH 2.0 [46]. Our findings suggest that the VMA reaction mechanism may comprise of two successive single-electron transfers as shown in Scheme 3B (route I). The overall oxidation mechanism of VMA occurs in the o-methoxyphenols position with a loss of two-electron and two protons process yielding the corresponding o-quinone and releasing CH3OH [47]. The first part involves the formation of phenoxyl radical cation by a one-electron oxidation (P1), followed by loss one proton to form phenoxyl radical and then a one-electron oxidation (P2) to form carbocation species at the aromatic ring. It is possible too that carbocation will likely be hydrolyzed leading to the loss of methanol and the formation of the substituted o-quinone species [47] as shown in Scheme 3B (route II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor characteristics, detection limit, and analytical performance
ce:section.ce:para: For facile and ready-to-use simultaneous analysis of 5-HIAA and VMA, we propose SWV detection on dual-imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD. Calibration curves are the result of optimized SWV conditions (potential step = 25 mV, SW amplitude = 20 mV and frequency = 5 Hz) across a potential range of 0.0 to +1.0 V. As can be seen in Fig. 3 A, the GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE leads to characteristic anodic peaks of 5-HIAA at +0.45 V and VMA at +0.62 V (P1) and +0.78 V (P2), respectively. The peak of 5-HIAA at +0.45 V arises from oxidation of the phenolic hydroxyl group to form a quinone-type species, while VMA oxidation peaks at +0.62 V (P1) and +0.78 V (P2) correspond to two successive one-electron transfers mechanisms (Scheme 3). The corresponding GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGrE sensor characteristics reveal linear responses for both 5-HIAA and VMA over 0.001–25 μM (Fig. 3B and C). The limits of detection (LOD) for both target analytes, based on three times the standard deviation of the blank (3Sb, n = 5), are 0.023 nM for 5-HIAA and 0.047 nM for VMA. The results reveal that the developed Origami 3D-ePAD is highly sensitive. Interestingly, the limit of quantification (LOQ) estimated from the lowest concentration is 1 nM, for both 5-HIAA and VMA, which is suitable for urine and plasma samples.

Table 1 compares the analytical performances of the developed Origami 3D-ePAD with previous related nanocomposite-modified electrodes for detecting 5-HIAA and VMA. The performance of our dual-imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD is indeed better than those previously reported: the sensor offers the lowest LODs for VMA (0.047 nM). Additionally, it detects VMA with the highest performance in terms of sensitivity compared to all other methods collected in Table 1. For 5-HIAA, our sensor offers a limit of detection as low as 0.023 nM and is better than those previously reported (Table 1). One sensor using a molecularly imprinted polypyrrole -modified glassy carbon electrode (MIPPy/GCE) [54] gives lower LOD (15 pM) than ours (0.023 nM). That work [54] comprises an electropolymerized pyrrole on the GCE surface, which somewhat limits the application by the long-time synthesis. Furthermore, the process is not convenient for mass production.

Last, but not least, GQDs@dual-MIP core-shell offers good mechanical stability and cost-effectivity with straightforward synthesis (∼250 mg/batch). The Origami 3D-ePAD can be readily prepared by hand screen-printing in a short time with suitable for mass production at a reasonable price (∼1.8 Euro/6 sensors). Our sensor provides several advantages: an artificial recognition element including physical and chemical robustness, and an approach substantially decreasing the analysis time.

For interference tests, we assessed compounds usually present in urine and plasma as well as drugs or compounds with similar structures as 5-HIAA. We calculated the value of the imprinting factor (IF) which measures the strength of the interaction of the imprinted polymer towards the template molecule [40,41]. The highest IF value indicates the successful construction of imprinting sites. Fig. 4 shows responses for the MIP sensor (front row, pink bar) and NIP sensor (middle row, green bar) when exposed to solutions containing the respective 5-HIAA or VMA or other substances at a concentration of 10 μM. The IF values (Fig. 4, back row, blue bar) for 5-HIAA and VMA are 8.46 and 7.10, respectively. For other compounds, the IF values are very low in the range of 0.86–1.80. The IF value for both target molecules is significantly larger than that of competing compounds. These findings clearly show that the designed sensor is highly selective as a consequence of the imprinted cavities in the material. As a result, only 5-HIAA and VMA molecules can attach to specific polymer dual recognition sites. Other interfering compounds with structures differing from the 5-HIAA and VMA are unable to bind firmly to the recognition sites.

We also evaluated the tolerance limits of the sensors toward these interfering compounds to find the maximum concentration that results in less than ±5 % variation of the oxidation peak current of 5-HIAA or VMA standards, respectively. The results indicate that there is no significant interference: the dual-imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD tolerates most test substances commonly found in urine and plasma samples in range of 15- to 250-fold (i.e., tolerate 15-fold of HVA, 25-fold of 4-NQO and ascorbic acid, 100-fold of guanosine and uric acid, 150-fold of citric acid, 200-fold of 3-NT and a 250-fold of creatinine, glucose, urea, and NaCl). This proves appreciable selectivity of the fabricated sensor towards simultaneous 5-HIAA and VMA detection and the suitability for applying in urine and plasma samples.

Reproducibility is a critical feature for practical application of the Origami 3D-ePAD's. We investigated the repeatability of manufacturing by comparing the current responses of five different devices toward 5-HIAA and VMA at a concentration of 10 μM each. The results (Figure S6A and S6B) show that all of the Origami 3D-ePADs provided similar response currents, with an RSD of 3.74 % for 5-HIAA and 4.37 % for VMA. These findings imply that the fabrication process is very reliable and uniform. Moreover, we evaluated the stability of the Origami 3D-ePAD by monitoring the current signals at various intervals over four weeks. During the first day of study period, five repeated measurements were conducted and the mean current value was considered as the reference giving 100 % of the analytical performance. The action limit was defined as ± 3SD and drawn on both sides of the mean value as dotted lines (Figures S6C and S6D). By setting the action limit, Origami 3D-ePAD was considered as stable from analytical aspect until the current control results was over the ± 3SD which is fixed as the rejection stability criterion [54]. The results (Figures S6C and S6D) demonstrated that after 21 days, the signal of the device retains 95.2 % (5-HIAA) and 86.9 % (VMA) of their initial oxidation current. The device was considered as stable and did not show any noticeable deterioration of analytical performance over almost three weeks (21 days). These results confirm that the dual-imprinted Origami shows good reproducibility, and stability, demonstrating the ability to work with high sensitivity and selectivity with high potential for application in point-of-care analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and applicability for clinical diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine and plasma samples from healthy persons as well as cancer patients' plasma were analyzed using our dual-imprinted Origami 3D-ePAD to demonstrate its usability for clinical diagnosis at the point of care. HPLC coupled with a fluorescence detector (HPLC-FLD) set excitation at 280 nm and emission at 315 nm, and this was employed to validate the data. Table S1 summarizes the results of this approach: the recovery rates (i.e., 5-HIAA ranged from 82.98 to 98.40 %, and VMA ranged from 83.28 to 104.39 %) of the Origami 3D-ePAD fall in an acceptable range, indicating no significant matrix interferences in urine analysis. The RSD value of less than 4.37 % obtained from the Origami 3D-ePAD demonstrates appreciable repeatability. The results obtained from fluorescent detection agree well with HPLC data, with deviations being 4.5 % or less. Hence, the proposed Origami 3D-ePAD is sustainable, promising, and applicable for detecting real samples, with high reliability and accuracy.

As shown in Table S2, the initial concentrations in the healthy persons ranged from 0.048 to 0.061 μM for 5-HIAA and 0.024–0.083 μM for VMA. In the cancer patients, the range was from 0.209 to 0.259 μM for 5-HIAA and 0.210–0.280 μM for VMA. Our developed method shows recoveries and % RSD within an acceptable range. Hence our Origami 3D-ePAD is sufficiently accurate and precise. The analysis is highly selective without any evidence of matrix interference.

A novel dual-imprinted sensor based on an Origami 3D-ePAD for simultaneous determination of 5-HIAA and VMA was successfully manufactured and applied to assess these cancer biomarkers in urine and plasma samples. It demonstrated the device's usability for clinical diagnosis at the point of care. We create the dual imprinted VMA and 5-HIAA shell on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via one-pot synthesis using 3-(3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) functional monomer and co-polymerization between APTES and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS). We fabricated a facile and ready-to-use Origami 3D-ePAD using a GQDs@dual-MIP modified graphene electrode, GQDs@dual-MIP/SPGE. The GQDs@dual-MIP core shell can detect the target tumor biomarkers with high catalytic activity, selectivity, and excellent conductivity. The increase in anodic peak current achieved from the Origami 3D-ePAD is because of recognition cavities fabricated on the core surface of GQDs. The dual analytes can diffuse to the electrode surface through the imprinting sites or recognition cavities, enhancing the transfer of electrons between analyte molecules and the electrode surface, and modulating the electrical signal. Our approach provides sensing performance while being ready-to-use, cost-effective, simple to synthesize, flexible, mechanically stable, and suitable for large-scale manufacturing. The Origami 3D-ePAD has an appreciable linearity range for both 5-HIAA and VMA (0.001–25 μM) and low detection limits (0.023 nM for 5-HIAA and 0.047 nM for VMA). Performance in urine analysis of our device validated with HPLC results. Our Origami 3D-ePAD exhibits high accuracy, high stability, and good performance without any evidence of matrix interferences when applied to determining 5-HIAA and VMA in human plasma from healthy persons and cancer patients. These characteristics result in outstanding selectivity, sensitivity, rapid analysis, cost-effectiveness, and affordability. Our Origami 3D-ePAD is well-suited as an alternative platform for point-of-care analysis.
